# **Gobuster: The Basics premium**  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Throughout this room, we will be using the AttackBox, where is already installed, to enumerate the web server directories and subdomains. However, if you prefer to use your own machine instead of the AttackBox, you must be connected to the TryHackMe and have installed. You can find installation instructions for on your own machine in the official [ GitHub gobuster repository (opens in new tab)](https://github.com/OJ/gobuster "https://github.com/OJ/gobuster").  
STEP1  
You can start the web server by clicking the Start Machine  button below. The VM will take approximately 2 minutes to boot up.  Direct access to this web server is not required. To start the  AttackBox, click the Start AttackBox button at the top of the page.  
Important: We work in a local network with a DNS server on the web  server. To ensure we can resolve the domains used throughout this room,  you need to change the /etc/resolv-dnsmasq file:  
- Open up a terminal on the the AttackBox and enter the command: sudo nano /etc/resolv-dnsmasq.  
- Insert nameserver 10.48.177.156 as the first line.  
- Save the file by pressing CTRL+O, followed by pressing ENTER, and then exit the editor by pressing CTRL+X.  
- Enter the command /etc/init.d/dnsmasq restart to restart the Dnsmasq service.  
The file should look something like this:  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Again  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Gobuster  
is an open-source offensive tool written in Golang. It enumerates web directories,  subdomains, vhosts, Amazon   buckets, and Google Cloud Storage by brute force, using specific  wordlists and handling the incoming responses. Many security  professionals use this tool for penetration testing, bug bounty hunting,  and cyber security assessments. Looking at the phases of ethical  hacking, we can place   
 between the reconnaissance and scanning phases.  
Before exploring   
, let’s briefly discuss the concepts of enumeration and Brute Force.  
Enumeration   
Enumeration is the act of listing all the available resources, whether they are accessible or not. For example,   
 enumerates web directories.  
Brute Force   
Brute force is the act of trying  every possibility until a match is found. It is like having ten keys and  trying them all on a lock until one fits.   
 uses wordlists for this purpose.  
## **Example**  
Let us look at an example of how we would use these commands and flags together to enumerate a web directory:  
gobuster dir -u "http://www.example.thm/" -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/small.txt -t 64  
- gobuster dir indicates that we will use the directory and file enumeration mode.  
- -u "http://www.example.thm/" tells Gobuster that the target URL is   
[://example.](http://example.thm/ "http://example.thm/")  
- [/ (opens in new tab)](http://example.thm/ "http://example.thm/").  
- -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/small.txt directs   
 to use the *small.txt* wordlist to brute force the web directories. will use each entry in the wordlist to form a new URL and send a GET request to that URL. If the first entry of the wordlist were images,   
-  would send a GET request to [http://example.thm/images/. (opens in new tab)](http://example.thm/images/ "http://example.thm/images/")  
- -t 64 sets the number of threads   
 will use to 64. This improves th  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Answers –d  
Asnwer dns  
   
### **Gobuster dir mode**  
- **Gobuster dir** is used to  **enumerate directories and files** on a website   
-  It uses a **wordlist** to brute-force possible paths   
-  It returns **status codes** (e.g., 200, 301, 403) to show what exists   
-  Common use: **finding hidden endpoints during pentesting**  
**Key flags:**  
- -u → target URL   
- -w → wordlist   
- -x → file extensions (e.g., .php,.js)   
- -k → skip TLS verification   
- -r → follow redirects   
- ![](data:image/png;base64,)  
### **Answers + Commands (Copy & Use)**  
#### ***1. Flag to skip TLS verification (long format)***  
--no-tls-validation  
   
#### ***2. Enumerate directories***  
gobuster dir -u http://www.offensivetools.thm -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -k  
👉 From the results, look for an **interesting directory** (e.g., /admin, /assets, /dev, etc.)  
ANSWER** assert**  
#### ***3. Enumerate inside the discovered directory + find .js file***  
(Example: if you found /assets)  
   
gobuster dir -u http://www.offensivetools.thm/assets -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -x .js -k  
   
#### ***4. Open the .js file to get the flag***  
curl http://www.offensivetools.thm/assets/<filename>.js  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
AGAIN

ans → -d

![](data:image/png;base64,)  
ans → 4  
**Task 6:**Use Case: Vhost Enumeration  
Q1.Use the commands learned in this task to answer the following question: How many vhosts on the offensivetools.thm domain reply with a status code 200?  
ans → 4  
**Task 7:**Conclusion  
ans → No answer needed  
   
### **Virtual Hosts (VHOST) — Simple Explanation**  
**Virtual hosts** are  **multiple websites running on the same server (same IP address)**.  
### **Example**  
One IP:  
10.48.177.156  
But it can host:  
www.offensivetools.thm  
 admin.offensivetools.thm  
 dev.offensivetools.thm  
 blog.offensivetools.thm  
👉 All these are **different websites**, but  **same IP**  
### **How it works (important)**  
When you open a site, your browser sends a request like:  
GET / HTTP/1.1  
 Host: www.offensivetools.thm  
👉 The **Host header** tells the server:  
“Which website do you want from this IP?”  
### **Why it matters in hacking**  
-  Some websites are **hidden (not public)**  
-  Example:   
- www.site.thm → normal site   
- admin.site.thm → admin panel (hidden)   
👉 Gobuster vhost tries to **guess these hidden hosts**  
   
4
